10 Testing Patterns — The Editorial Review Process

← Back to lessons

Tests are the editorial review process for code. They catch errors before publication, verify that changes don't break existing stories, and give you confidence to make bold edits. This lesson covers testing TypeScript code with Vitest.

The Mental Model: Journalism's Review Layers

A newspaper has multiple review layers before publication:

  • Unit tests = fact-checking individual claims. Does this function return the right value for these inputs?
  • Integration tests = editorial review of a complete section. Do these modules work together correctly?
  • End-to-end tests = printing a test run of the whole paper. Does the final product look right from the reader's perspective?

You need all three layers, but the ratio matters. The testing pyramid: many unit tests (fast, cheap), fewer integration tests (medium), very few E2E tests (slow, expensive).

Vitest: The Modern Test Runner

Vitest is the testing framework built for Vite projects. It's Jest-compatible but faster, with native TypeScript support.

// Install
npm install -D vitest

// vite.config.ts (or vitest.config.ts)
import { defineConfig } from 'vitest/config';

export default defineConfig({
  test: {
    globals: true,        // describe, it, expect without imports
    environment: 'node',  // or 'jsdom' for browser APIs
    coverage: {
      provider: 'v8',
      reporter: ['text', 'html'],
    },
  },
});

// package.json
{
  "scripts": {
    "test": "vitest",
    "test:run": "vitest run",
    "test:coverage": "vitest run --coverage"
  }
}

Your First Test

// src/utils/format.ts
export function formatCurrency(cents: number): string {
  return `$${(cents / 100).toFixed(2)}`;
}

// src/utils/format.test.ts
import { describe, it, expect } from 'vitest';
import { formatCurrency } from './format';

describe('formatCurrency', () => {
  it('formats cents to dollar string', () => {
    expect(formatCurrency(1099)).toBe('$10.99');
  });

  it('handles zero', () => {
    expect(formatCurrency(0)).toBe('$0.00');
  });

  it('handles negative amounts', () => {
    expect(formatCurrency(-500)).toBe('$-5.00');
  });
});

Anatomy of a Good Test

Every test follows the AAA pattern: Arrange, Act, Assert.

it('publishes a draft article', () => {
  // ARRANGE — set up the scenario
  const article = createArticle({ status: 'draft', title: 'Breaking News' });
  const publisher = new Publisher();

  // ACT — do the thing
  const result = publisher.publish(article);

  // ASSERT — verify the outcome
  expect(result.status).toBe('published');
  expect(result.publishedAt).toBeInstanceOf(Date);
  expect(result.title).toBe('Breaking News');
});

Test names should read like headlines. A journalist writes headlines that tell you the story without reading the article. A test name should tell you the expected behavior without reading the code: "publishes a draft article," not "test publish."

Common Matchers

// Equality
expect(value).toBe(42);              // Strict equality (===)
expect(obj).toEqual({ a: 1 });       // Deep equality
expect(obj).toStrictEqual({ a: 1 }); // Deep + no extra properties

// Truthiness
expect(value).toBeTruthy();
expect(value).toBeFalsy();
expect(value).toBeNull();
expect(value).toBeUndefined();
expect(value).toBeDefined();

// Numbers
expect(value).toBeGreaterThan(10);
expect(value).toBeCloseTo(0.3, 5);   // Float comparison

// Strings
expect(str).toMatch(/regex/);
expect(str).toContain("substring");

// Arrays
expect(arr).toContain(item);
expect(arr).toHaveLength(3);

// Objects
expect(obj).toHaveProperty('key');
expect(obj).toMatchObject({ partial: true });

// Exceptions
expect(() => dangerousCall()).toThrow();
expect(() => dangerousCall()).toThrow('specific message');
expect(() => dangerousCall()).toThrowError(TypeError);

Mocking

Mocks replace real dependencies with controlled substitutes. Like using a source who gives you exactly the quotes you need for a test story.

Function Mocks

import { vi, describe, it, expect } from 'vitest';

// Create a mock function
const mockFetch = vi.fn();

// Configure return values
mockFetch.mockResolvedValue({
  ok: true,
  json: () => Promise.resolve({ articles: [] }),
});

// Use in test
it('fetches articles', async () => {
  const api = new ApiClient(mockFetch);
  const articles = await api.getArticles();

  // Verify the mock was called correctly
  expect(mockFetch).toHaveBeenCalledWith('/api/articles', expect.objectContaining({
    method: 'GET',
  }));
  expect(mockFetch).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);
  expect(articles).toEqual([]);
});

Module Mocks

// Mock an entire module
vi.mock('./database', () => ({
  query: vi.fn().mockResolvedValue([{ id: 1, title: 'Test' }]),
  connect: vi.fn(),
}));

// The real database module is never loaded
import { query } from './database';

it('queries the database', async () => {
  const results = await query('SELECT * FROM articles');
  expect(results).toHaveLength(1);
});

Spy on Existing Methods

it('logs errors to console', () => {
  const consoleSpy = vi.spyOn(console, 'error').mockImplementation(() => {});

  handleError(new Error('test'));

  expect(consoleSpy).toHaveBeenCalledWith(
    expect.stringContaining('test')
  );

  consoleSpy.mockRestore();  // Clean up
});

Mock sparingly. Over-mocking makes tests brittle and disconnected from reality. A good rule: mock I/O boundaries (network, filesystem, database) and time. Don't mock your own business logic — test it directly.

Testing Async Code

// Vitest handles async/await natively
it('fetches user data', async () => {
  const user = await fetchUser(1);
  expect(user.name).toBe('Trevor');
});

// Testing rejected promises
it('throws on invalid ID', async () => {
  await expect(fetchUser(-1)).rejects.toThrow('Invalid ID');
});

// Testing with timers
it('debounces search input', async () => {
  vi.useFakeTimers();

  const search = vi.fn();
  const debounced = debounce(search, 300);

  debounced('hello');
  debounced('hello world');

  expect(search).not.toHaveBeenCalled();

  vi.advanceTimersByTime(300);

  expect(search).toHaveBeenCalledOnce();
  expect(search).toHaveBeenCalledWith('hello world');

  vi.useRealTimers();
});

Snapshot Testing

// Snapshot testing captures output and compares to a saved version
it('generates correct HTML', () => {
  const html = renderArticle({ title: 'Test', body: 'Content' });
  expect(html).toMatchSnapshot();
  // First run: saves snapshot to __snapshots__/
  // Subsequent runs: compares to saved snapshot
});

// Inline snapshots (stored in the test file)
it('formats date correctly', () => {
  const formatted = formatDate(new Date('2024-01-15'));
  expect(formatted).toMatchInlineSnapshot(`"January 15, 2024"`);
});

Snapshot anti-pattern: Don't snapshot large, frequently changing objects. Snapshots work best for small, stable outputs (formatted strings, rendered HTML fragments, serialized config). Large snapshots become "approve everything" exercises that catch nothing.

TDD Workflow

Test-Driven Development is a three-step cycle: Red, Green, Refactor.

// Step 1: RED — Write a failing test
it('validates email format', () => {
  expect(isValidEmail('trevor@example.com')).toBe(true);
  expect(isValidEmail('not-an-email')).toBe(false);
  expect(isValidEmail('')).toBe(false);
});

// Step 2: GREEN — Write the minimum code to pass
function isValidEmail(email: string): boolean {
  return /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/.test(email);
}

// Step 3: REFACTOR — Improve the code without changing behavior
// Tests stay green, proving the refactor is safe
const EMAIL_REGEX = /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/;
function isValidEmail(email: string): boolean {
  return EMAIL_REGEX.test(email);
}

TDD is an investigation technique. A journalist outlines questions before the interview. TDD outlines expected behavior before writing code. The test is your hypothesis; the implementation is your proof. This discipline prevents scope creep and ensures every line of code has a reason to exist.

Testing TypeScript-Specific Patterns

// Testing type guards
import { describe, it, expect } from 'vitest';

type Article = { type: 'article'; title: string; body: string };
type Video = { type: 'video'; title: string; duration: number };
type Content = Article | Video;

function isArticle(content: Content): content is Article {
  return content.type === 'article';
}

describe('isArticle', () => {
  it('returns true for articles', () => {
    const article: Content = { type: 'article', title: 'Test', body: 'Body' };
    expect(isArticle(article)).toBe(true);
  });

  it('returns false for videos', () => {
    const video: Content = { type: 'video', title: 'Test', duration: 120 };
    expect(isArticle(video)).toBe(false);
  });

  it('narrows the type correctly', () => {
    const content: Content = { type: 'article', title: 'Test', body: 'Body' };
    if (isArticle(content)) {
      // TypeScript knows content is Article here
      expect(content.body).toBe('Body');
    }
  });
});

// Testing generic functions
describe('groupBy', () => {
  function groupBy<T, K extends string>(items: T[], keyFn: (item: T) => K): Record<K, T[]> {
    return items.reduce((acc, item) => {
      const key = keyFn(item);
      (acc[key] = acc[key] || []).push(item);
      return acc;
    }, {} as Record<K, T[]>);
  }

  it('groups items by key', () => {
    const articles = [
      { title: 'A', status: 'draft' as const },
      { title: 'B', status: 'published' as const },
      { title: 'C', status: 'draft' as const },
    ];

    const grouped = groupBy(articles, a => a.status);

    expect(grouped.draft).toHaveLength(2);
    expect(grouped.published).toHaveLength(1);
  });
});

Knowledge Check

1. What's the difference between toBe and toEqual?

toBe uses strict equality (===) — same reference for objects. toEqual performs deep equality — compares structure and values recursively. Use toBe for primitives and reference checks. Use toEqual for objects and arrays.

2. When should you use vi.fn() vs vi.spyOn()?

vi.fn() creates a standalone mock function with no original implementation. Use it when passing a callback to the code under test. vi.spyOn() wraps an existing method, preserving the original implementation by default (unless you .mockImplementation()). Use it when you want to observe calls to an existing method or temporarily override it.

3. Why does the testing pyramid have more unit tests than E2E tests?

Unit tests are fast (milliseconds), reliable (no external dependencies), and precise (failures point to exact functions). E2E tests are slow (seconds/minutes), flaky (network, browser, timing), and vague (failures require investigation). Unit tests give fast feedback during development. E2E tests verify the whole system works but are expensive to maintain.

Exercise: TDD an Article Validator

Use TDD (write tests FIRST) to build an ArticleValidator class:

// Write tests for ALL of these rules, then implement:

// 1. Title must be 5-100 characters
// 2. Body must be at least 50 characters
// 3. Status must be "draft", "published", or "archived"
// 4. If status is "published", publishedAt must be a valid Date
// 5. Tags must be an array of 1-5 non-empty strings

interface Article {
  title: string;
  body: string;
  status: "draft" | "published" | "archived";
  publishedAt?: Date;
  tags: string[];
}

type ValidationResult =
  | { valid: true; article: Article }
  | { valid: false; errors: string[] };

class ArticleValidator {
  validate(input: unknown): ValidationResult { ... }
}

Process: Write one test, see it fail (red), implement just enough to pass (green), refactor, repeat.
